sections.title: Feedback from the students of Slovanské Gymnázium on the Digital Fairness Act.
path: Feedback from the students of Slovanské Gymnázium on the Digital Fairness Act.

sections.title: 7. Protection for Children and Young Users
path: 7. Protection for Children and Young Users

sections.title: 8. One Set of EU Rules for Everyone
path: 8. One Set of EU Rules for Everyone

section_path: Feedback from the students of Slovanské Gymnázium on the Digital Fairness Act.
paragraphs.text: Students broadly welcomed the proposed Digital Fairness Act and its goal to make online life safer, fairer, and more transparent. Across all areas, they supported stronger regulation to protect users, but often questioned how effectively such provisions could be enforced in practice. They felt this act was a thoughtful and solid proposal in comparison to the recent Chat Control proposal, which they strongly opposed.

Students supported bans on manipulative design tactics such as hidden buttons, misleading pop-ups, and forced consent. They appreciated the focus on honesty and user autonomy. However, some students with coding experience doubted whether addictive or manipulative elements can ever be fully eliminated, as they are embedded in modern interface design. Many agreed adults bear some responsibility for their online choices but insisted users under 15 should have far stronger protections.

This provision generated lively debate. Students agreed on the importance of transparency about spending and screen time, especially in video games. They proposed that games display total money spent in real currency and total hours played. While most supported restrictions on addictive features like infinite scrolling or daily rewards, some feared such limits might harm the enjoyment of games. Students also proposed banning addictive design for products linked to harmful goods such as tobacco, alcohol, energy drinks, and gambling apps.

This section was among the most popular. Students opposed the use of personal data to manipulate or exploit users and strongly supported the right to opt out of personalised advertising. They were particularly concerned about emotional targeting — for example, adverts exploiting loneliness, insecurity, or sadness. All students agreed that personalisation should be completely prohibited for users under 15. Some supported limited, transparent notifications for items users had voluntarily favoured.

This was the most discussed topic. Students unanimously supported clear labelling of paid content and stronger accountability for both influencers and the companies that sponsor them. They raised serious concerns about the promotion of potentially harmful or misleading products, noting that many influencers target young audiences with unrealistic or unsafe claims.

Female students highlighted beauty and health products such as collagen supplements, "hair vitamins," diet pills, detox teas, and aesthetic procedures (like filler injections or skin treatments) that promote unrealistic beauty standards or have unverified health effects.
Male students expressed concern about fitness influencers promoting excessive protein supplements, creatine, or pre-workout products that can be harmful when misused.
Many also mentioned energy drinks, gambling apps, and “get rich quick” investment schemes as problematic.

Students worried that younger audiences, especially teenagers, cannot always distinguish sponsored content from genuine recommendations. They suggested that platforms require clear visual disclaimers (e.g., “Paid Promotion”) and that repeated violations should result in account suspension or legal penalties.

Some students, particularly from Ukraine, also raised concerns about influencers spreading propaganda, disinformation, or politically biased content, and suggested that such misuse should carry legal consequences.
Students were unanimously positive about these provisions. They supported clear, upfront pricing, transparent discounts, and the end of hidden fees.

Students praised these measures as fair and necessary. They particularly supported the requirement for easy online cancellation and reminders before automatic renewals, as well as banning “free trials” that convert to paid plans without explicit consent.

section_path: 7. Protection for Children and Young Users
paragraphs.text: Students strongly endorsed protections for minors but emphasised that different rules should apply to different age groups, notably distinguishing between under-15s and those aged 15–18. They warned that overly intrusive parental controls or digital ID systems could harm vulnerable youths who rely on online spaces for community or self-expression. They called for a balance between safety, privacy, and creative freedom.

section_path: 8. One Set of EU Rules for Everyone
paragraphs.text: Students welcomed uniform EU-wide standards but questioned whether the EU can effectively communicate these new rules to the public. They stressed that awareness campaigns and accessible explanations in schools are essential for real impact.

section_path: 8. One Set of EU Rules for Everyone
paragraphs.text: Overall, students were optimistic about the Digital Fairness Act’s goals and spirit. They called for strong enforcement, clear public communication, and balanced measures that protect without restricting freedom. Their main concerns centred on practical enforcement, feasibility, and ensuring that young people remain both protected and empowered online.
